hints.concrete: Check tens first using the place value blocks.
hints.strategy: If tens are tied, compare ones.
hints.worked: 32 vs 38: both have 3 tens, so compare ones, and 2 is less than 8.

hints.concrete: Use a place-value chart for hundreds, tens, and ones.
hints.strategy: Compare hundreds first. If tied, compare tens.
hints.worked: Line up digits by place value and compare step by step.

hints.concrete: Use a place-value chart for tenths/hundredths.
hints.strategy: Compare tenths first, then hundredths.
hints.worked: Rewrite with equal decimal places and compare digits.

hints.concrete: Draw fraction strips with equal-sized wholes.
hints.strategy: Find common denominators or compare to benchmark 1/2.
hints.worked: Convert each fraction to a common denominator and compare.

hints.concrete: Partition the whole into equal groups first.
hints.strategy: Find one part, then scale by numerator.
hints.worked: Compute whole ÷ denominator, then multiply by numerator.

hints.concrete: Build rows and columns with counters.
hints.strategy: Use repeated addition from the array.
hints.worked: Count one row, then multiply by number of rows.

hints.concrete: Use counters and take away one at a time.
hints.strategy: Start at the big number and count back.
hints.worked: If it says 9 minus 4, count back four: 8, 7, 6, 5.

hints.concrete: Use a ten-frame or number line to see the jumps.
hints.strategy: Make ten first, then subtract the rest.
hints.worked: 14 minus 6 can be 14 minus 4 equals 10, then minus 2 equals 8.

hints.concrete: Place long rods in Tens and little cubes in Ones.
hints.strategy: Two-digit numbers are tens plus ones.
hints.worked: 47 means 4 tens and 7 ones.

hints.concrete: Think of layers of unit cubes.
hints.strategy: Find area of one layer, then multiply by height.
hints.worked: Volume = length × width × height.

hints.concrete: Point to each dot as you count.
hints.strategy: Start from one corner and count in order.
hints.worked: Count each dot: 1, 2, 3... the answer is the last number you say.

hints.concrete: Use a ten frame to fill in the missing part.
hints.strategy: Think: what number plus the given number equals 10?
hints.worked: If one part is 7, count up from 7 to 10: 8, 9, 10 — that's 3 more.

hints.concrete: Use your fingers or counters to add.
hints.strategy: Count on from the bigger number.
hints.worked: For 2 + 1: start at 2, count 1 more: 3.

hints.concrete: Use a ten frame to show both groups.
hints.strategy: Count on from the bigger number.
hints.worked: For 4 + 3: start at 4, count 5, 6, 7.

hints.concrete: Use a number line to hop forward.
hints.strategy: Make a ten first, then add the rest.

hints.concrete: Use a number bond triangle.
hints.strategy: Think: whole minus known part = missing part.
hints.worked: For 6 + ? = 9: 9 - 6 = 3, so the missing part is 3.

hints.concrete: Use base-ten blocks: tens sticks and ones cubes.
hints.strategy: Add ones first, then tens. Regroup if ones sum is 10 or more.
hints.worked: For 47 + 36: 7 + 6 = 13, write 3 carry 1. 4 + 3 + 1 = 8. Answer: 83.

hints.concrete: Use base-ten blocks. Trade a ten for 10 ones if needed.
hints.strategy: Subtract ones first. If you can't, regroup a ten.
hints.worked: For 72 - 45: Can't do 2 - 5, so regroup: 6 tens 12 ones. 12 - 5 = 7, 6 - 4 = 2. Answer: 27.

hints.concrete: Line up objects side by side to compare.
hints.strategy: Count each group, then see which number is bigger.
hints.worked: 5 stars vs 3 stars: 5 is more than 3, so the stars group has more.

hints.concrete: Trace the shape with your finger. Count corners and sides.
hints.strategy: Corners are where two sides meet. Count the straight sides.
hints.worked: A square has 4 straight sides and 4 corners.

hints.concrete: Use a hundreds chart or base-ten blocks.
hints.strategy: Add tens first, then ones. For subtraction, subtract tens first.
hints.worked: 34 + 22: add 20 to get 54, then add 2 to get 56.

hints.concrete: Line up objects end-to-end along the item.
hints.strategy: Start at one end and count each unit to the other end.
hints.worked: The pencil spans 7 paper clips, so it is 7 units long.

hints.concrete: Use hundreds flats, tens rods, and ones cubes.
hints.strategy: The hundreds digit tells how many hundreds. Look at each place.
hints.worked: In 452: 4 is in the hundreds place, 5 in tens, 2 in ones.

hints.concrete: Use base-ten blocks and trade 10 ones for 1 ten.
hints.strategy: Add ones first. If sum is 10+, carry to tens column.

hints.concrete: Draw circles for groups and dots inside each.
hints.strategy: Equal groups means same number in each. Multiply groups × amount.

hints.concrete: Use a clock model with movable hands.
hints.strategy: Short hand = hours. Long hand = minutes. Count by 5s.
hints.worked: Short hand on 3, long hand on 6: that's 3:30.

hints.concrete: Look at the bars — taller means more.
hints.strategy: Read the number at the top of each bar.
hints.worked: If the Red bar reaches 5 and Blue reaches 3, Red has 2 more.

hints.concrete: Deal objects into equal groups like dealing cards.
hints.strategy: Think: what number times divisor equals dividend?
hints.worked: 12 ÷ 3: deal 12 into 3 groups — each gets 4.

hints.concrete: Split the whole into equal parts, then count.
hints.strategy: Divide the whole by denominator, multiply by numerator.

hints.concrete: Draw fraction strips with equal-sized wholes.
hints.strategy: Find common denominators to compare directly.
hints.worked: Compare 2/3 and 3/4: 8/12 vs 9/12. 3/4 is larger.

hints.concrete: Cover the rectangle with unit squares and count.
hints.strategy: Area = length × width.
hints.worked: A 4 by 3 rectangle has 4×3=12 square units of area.

hints.concrete: Break the problem into smaller steps.
hints.strategy: Do one operation at a time. Check your work.

hints.concrete: Use a place-value chart up to millions.
hints.strategy: Each place is 10 times the one to its right.
hints.worked: In 350,000 the 5 is in ten-thousands, so its value is 50,000.

hints.concrete: Use an area model to break numbers apart.
hints.strategy: Multiply ones first, then tens, then add partial products.

hints.concrete: Subtract groups from the dividend step by step.
hints.strategy: Start with the largest easy multiple you can subtract.

hints.concrete: Use fraction strips with matching parts.
hints.strategy: Same denominators: just add or subtract the numerators.
hints.worked: 1/4 + 2/4 = 3/4. Keep the denominator, add numerators.

hints.concrete: Use a protractor — line up the base line.
hints.strategy: Acute < 90°, Right = 90°, Obtuse > 90°.
hints.worked: A 120° angle is obtuse because it is greater than 90°.

hints.concrete: Find a common denominator using fraction strips.
hints.strategy: Convert to equivalent fractions with the LCD, then add/subtract.

hints.concrete: Each X on the line plot represents one item.
hints.strategy: Count the Xs above each fraction to read the data.
hints.worked: If 3 Xs sit above 1/4, then 3 items measure 1/4 inch.

hints.concrete: Use a ratio table: write the pattern and extend it.
hints.strategy: Find the multiplier between known and unknown.
hints.worked: 2 apples cost 6¢. 4 apples is 2×, so 6×2=12¢.
